coredata.dc:title: Synthesizing anode electrochemiluminescent self-catalyzed carbon dots-based nanocomposites and its application in sensitive ECL biosensor for microRNA detection
coredata.dc:creator: Zhong, Xia
Li, Xue
Zhuo, Ying
Chai, Yaqin
Yuan, Ruo
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel self-catalyzed carbon dots-based electrochemiluminescence (ECL) luminophore was prepared by loading the amino modified carbon dots onto the surface of AuNPs modified TiO2NPs (CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2) and applied in sensitive ECL biosensor for miRNA-21 detection. Significantly, the prepared CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2 nanocomposites exhibited strong initial ECL intensity for the acceleration of TiO2NPs towards to the electrochemical reduction of co-reactive H2O2, addressing the shortcomings of the strong oxidizing nature of persulfate in traditional CDs-based ECL biosensings and enhancing the detection sensitivity. Moreover, by target-induced catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) amplification, a large number of quenching groups could be released and captured on the electrode surface, further improving the detection sensitivity. As a consequence, the ECL response of the proposed biosensor gradually decreased as the concentration of miRNA-21 increasing from 0.1 fM to 10 pM with the detection limit of 0.03 fM. The strategy provided in this work opens up a new way for miRNA detection in actual cell using self-catalyzed carbon dots-based ECL biosensing platform.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Electrochemiluminescence biosensor
Carbon dots
MicroRNA detection
Catalytic hairpin assembly

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Samples preparation for native polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE)
Synthesis of Au-PEI@TiO2 and CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2 nanocomposites
Fabrication of the biosensor and ECL measurements
PAGE characterization of the miRNA-21 triggered CHA process
Characterization of TiO2, CDs and CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2
ECL and EIS stepwise characterization of the biosensor assembly process
The ECL performance of the biosensor
Application of the biosensor in cancer cells
The stability and selectivity analysis of the biosensor

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation (NNSF) of China ( 21904108 , 21775124 , 21675129 ), the Natural Science Foundation Project of CQ CSTC ( cstc2018jcyjAX0546 ), the Chongqing Research Program of Basic Research and Frontier Technology ( cstc2017jcyjAX0230 ), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( XDJK2018C041 , XDJK2019TJ002 ), China. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The preparation of self-catalyzed carbon dots complex and the fabrication of the ECL biosensor assisted by CHA.

The PAGE analysis of miRNA-21 induced CHA. Lane 1: 1 μM H1; lane 2: 1 μM H2; lane 3: a mixture of 1 μM H1 and 1 μM H2; lane 4: 1 μM miRNA-21; lane 5: a mixture of 1 μM− H1, 1 μM− H2 and 1 μM⋅ miRNA-21. All the samples were heated to 95 °C for 5 min, and then cooled to 25 °C slowly.
The SEM characterization of TiO2 (A), Au-PEI@TiO2 (B), TEM graph of synthesized CDs (C), the fluorescence phenomenon (D, E) and emission spectra (F) of CDs and CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2, respectively.
The signal intensity of ECL (A): GCE + CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2 (a); GCE + CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2+H1 (b); GCE + CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2+H1+MCH (c); GCE+CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2+H1+MCH + miRNA-21/H2-DA (d); and EIS (B): bare GCE (a).
GCE + CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2 (b); GCE + CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2+H1 (c); GCE + CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2+H1+MCH (d); GCE + CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2+H1+MCH + miRNA-21/H2-DA (e).

(A) The ECL response of this biosensor for miRNA-21 detection at different concentration: (a) blank, (b) 0.1 fM, (c) 1 fM, (d) 10 fM, (e) 100 fM, (f) 1 pM, (g) 10 pM. (B) The calibration curve of the proposed biosensor for miRNA-21 detection. All of the ECL. response were detected in PBS 7.4 from 0 V to 0.8 V (vs Ag/AgCl) at a scanning rate of 100 mV⋅s−1. Error bars, SD, n = 3.
The ECL response of this biosensor for MCF-7 detection at different cell numbers. Error bars, SD, n = 3.
(A) The stability of this biosensor. (B) The selectivity of the proposed biosensor for different miRNAs and their mixture with miRNA-21. Error bars, SD, n = 3.

head.ce:title: Synthesizing anode electrochemiluminescent self-catalyzed carbon dots-based nanocomposites and its application in sensitive ECL biosensor for microRNA detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Luminescent and Real-Time Analytical Chemistry (Southwest University), Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel self-catalyzed carbon dots-based electrochemiluminescence (ECL) luminophore was prepared by loading the amino modified carbon dots onto the surface of AuNPs modified TiO2NPs (CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2) and applied in sensitive ECL biosensor for miRNA-21 detection. Significantly, the prepared CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2 nanocomposites exhibited strong initial ECL intensity for the acceleration of TiO2NPs towards to the electrochemical reduction of co-reactive H2O2, addressing the shortcomings of the strong oxidizing nature of persulfate in traditional CDs-based ECL biosensings and enhancing the detection sensitivity. Moreover, by target-induced catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) amplification, a large number of quenching groups could be released and captured on the electrode surface, further improving the detection sensitivity. As a consequence, the ECL response of the proposed biosensor gradually decreased as the concentration of miRNA-21 increasing from 0.1 fM to 10 pM with the detection limit of 0.03 fM. The strategy provided in this work opens up a new way for miRNA detection in actual cell using self-catalyzed carbon dots-based ECL biosensing platform.

ce:section.ce:para: Emissive Carbon dots (CDs), because of their low toxicity, good biocompatibility, simple synthesis method, good chemical stability, and adjustable emission wavelength, have gradually occupied the field of vision of researchers when carbon dots were first introduced [1–4]. It is mainly applied as a fluorescent probe in bioimaging to be widely used by researchers in biological analysis [5–7]. In recent years, the electrochemiluminescence properties of the carbon dots are gradually receiving attentions, and many research groups have begun to use carbon dots as novel luminescent reagents [8–10]. In these efforts, peroxodisulfate is typically used as carbon dot cathode ECL luminescence co-reaction reagent due to stable light emission and high ECL intensity [11,12]. However, the application of carbon dots in ECL field is limited by its disadvantages of the strong oxidizing property, high luminous potential of persulfate, etc [13–15]. In this work, simple hydrothermal synthesis method with the raw materials including citric acid and hydrated hydrazine was adopted to obtain CDs which could generate strong anode ECL signal at lower potentials with hydrogen peroxide as a co-reaction reagent, addressing the shortcomings of the strong oxidizing nature of persulfate.

As we all know, in order to improve the detection sensitivity in the construction of signal-off ECL biosensors, it is necessary to increase the initial ECL signal intensity as much as possible and the amount of quenching groups. Commonly, some nanomaterials are used as catalyst of luminescent reagents or as carriers to achieve large numbers of immobilization substrates for obtaining high initial signals, for example, titanium dioxide (TiO2), gold, platinum, palladium, and so on [16–18]. Among them, TiO2 was used as an ECL co-reaction accelerator in many reports [19,20]. What’s more, for acquiring better ECL performance, some reducing agents are often introduced in the synthesis of these nanomaterials to synthesize nanocomposites, such as citric acid, sodium borohydride and polyetherimide (PEI) [21,22]. In this work, the PEI, an amino-rich chain polymer, could not only be used as reducing agent, but also realize the in-situ reduction of Au onto the surface of TiO2 to obtain self-catalyzed CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2 complexes for efficient loading amounts of CDs.

In this work, novel ECL biosensor was constructed for sensitive detection of miRNA-21 based on the self-catalytic CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2 nanocomposites and target microRNA (miRNA)-induced CHA. As shown in Scheme 1 , we firstly synthesized a novel self-catalytic CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2 nanocomposites using PEI-coated TiO2 as a support by in-situ reduction to immobilize amounts of carbon dots to obtain high initial ECL signal. Then, the amino modified H1 was immobilized onto the electrode surface. In the presence of the target miRNA-21 and dopamine labeled H2 (H2-DA), H1 was opened by miRNA-21 to form the double-stranded structure of miRNA-21-H1, which in turn hybridized with H2-DA, allowing miRNAs-21 to be released into the free state for the next cycle. As a result, a large number of H1-H2-DA structures were produced on the electrode surface, significantly reducing the ECL signal. Moreover, as the concentration of miRNA-z21 increased, the ECL signal was gradually reduced, and finally a sensitive detection of miRNA-21 was achieved. In addition, the proposed ECL biosensor was successfully applied into actual cell experiments, demonstrating the potential application in clinic diagnose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Samples preparation for native polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (PAGE)
ce:section.ce:para: 2 μM H1 (sample 1) and 2 μM H2 (sample 2) were heated to 95 °C and kept 10 min to gradually cool to room temperature, respectively. Then, H1 and H2 were mixed in equal volumes (sample 3). 1 μM miRNA-21 was prepared by dilution of 100 μM miRNA-21 in DEPC water (sample 4). Afterwards, miRNA-21 was added into sample 3 to achieve a final concentration of 1 μM (sample 5). Next, sample 3 and 5 were incubated at 37 °C for 2 h. Finally, all the samples were loaded with 16% fresh polyacrylamide gel in 1 × TBE buffer for 2 h at 120 V. And the gel was stained with ethidium bromide for 20 min to get the gel imaging according by the Bio-Rad Gel Doc XR + System. The reagents and chemicals, apparatus have been provided in supplementary material.

The CDs were synthesized by citric acid and ethylenediamine using hydrothermal synthesis method with minor modifications [13]. Briefly, citric acid (1.75 g) was ultrasonically dispersed in the DMF (20 mL) in a beaker. After 5 min later, a homogeneous colorless solution was obtained. Then hydrazine hydrate (3 mL) was added with stirring, the colorless solution converted to ivory colored colloid solution. And then the mixture was transferred into a 100 mL autoclave in a Teflon cup and heated at 180 ℃ for 12 h. After cooling down to room temperature, the resulted brown CDs/DMF solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 10 min in order to remove the large particles residue of the solution.

For back extraction of the CDs to the CDs/DMF solution, dichloromethane and double distilled water (5 mL, respectively) were added in CDs/DMF solution. And the two-phase mixture was shaken in separating funnel and then settled at the room temperature, the dark brown upper layer solution and light brown lower layer solution was obtained. Then the lower layer solution was removed, and the remaining upper layer solution was extracted with the equal volume of dichloromethane and double distilled water again. The former steps were repeated six times, and the complete back-extraction was possible. The collected dark brown solution was dried at 60 ℃ in vacuum and washed for 3 times with ethanol. Finally, the dried product was dried again in vacuum at 60 ℃, and the viscous oil products of CDs was achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of Au-PEI@TiO2 and CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2 nanocomposites
ce:section.ce:para: The detailed preparation of CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2 nanocomposites was displayed in scheme 1 . Firstly, 5 mL PEI (1%) was prepared with 5 mL TiO2 nanoparticles solution according to the literature with stirring at room temperature for 12 h [23]. The mixture was centrifuged three times and dispersed in 10 mL deionized water to obtain PEI coated TiO2 (PEI@TiO2). After that, 10 mL PEI@TiO2 solution was transferred into a 50 mL beaker and heated to 70 °C. Then, 500 μL HAuCl4 was dropped into the PEI@TiO2 solution under stirring and heating until the solution turned purple red. The as-prepared Au-PEI@TiO2 was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the excess reagents. In addition, the sediments were dispersed into 2 mL deionized water. Finally, CDs was added into Au-PEI@TiO2 under stirring for 24 h. Then, the sediments were obtained by centrifuging and washing three times and dispersed in 2 mL deionized water for further use.

1 mL 3′-NH2 (100 μM) modified H2, 1 mL dopamine (DA, 10 μM) and 100 μL glutaraldehyde (DA, 1%, w/v) were reacted in room temperature under stirring for 4 h to obtain H2-DA by the crosslinking between NH2 in the dopamine and H2 with the help of DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the biosensor and ECL measurements
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, the bare glass carbon electrode (bare GCE) (Φ =4 mm) was carefully cleaned according to literature [24]. Then, 10 μL CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2 nanocomposites was dropped on GCE and dried to incubate 10 μL H1 and 5 μL GA at room temperature for 12 h. After rinsing with PBS, 10 μL MCH (1 mM) was dropped to block the nonspecific identification site.

Prior to measurement, 10 μL miRNA-21 and 10 μL H2-DA were incubated for 2 h at 37 °C. Then, the biosensor was injected in PBS containing 1 mM H2O2 for ECL detection from 0 to 0.8 V with a scanning rate of 100 mV/s at a PMT high-voltage of 800 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PAGE characterization of the miRNA-21 triggered CHA process
ce:section.ce:para: To proof the miRNA-21 triggered CHA process, the products of different stages were characterized by PAGE. As shown in Fig. 1 , the distinct bands of H1, H2 and miRNA-21 were observed in lane 1, lane 2 and lane 4, respectively, due to the different migration. The mixture of H1 and H2 showed two distinct bands in lane 3 with nearly identical mobility to single H1 and H2, respectively. When miRNA-21 was added into the mixture of H1 and H2, a highest single band was obtained in lane 5, owing to the hybridization between H1 and H2 triggered by miRNA-21. These results indicated that the miRNA-21 triggered CHA process was successful.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of TiO2, CDs and CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2
ce:section.ce:para: SEM was used to study the morphology of the TiO2 and Au-PEI@TiO2. The morphology of TiO2 exhibited relatively uniform nanoparticles with a diameter about 150 nm (Fig. 2 A). The Au-PEI@TiO2 was obtained by in situ reduction of HAuCl4 on the surface of PEI coated TiO2 nanoparticles. It could be seen that some small bright spots were decorated on PEI@TiO2, indicating the successful modification of AuNPs with large surface area and superior conductivity on the surface of PEI@TiO2 (Fig. 2B). Moreover, the TEM was employed to characterize the image of CDs, as shown in the Fig. 2C, the typical TEM image of CDs with homogeneous distribution was presented.

The images for the solutions of CDs and CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2 under visible-light irradiation and UV-light irradiation were displayed in Fig. 2D and 2E, respectively. Obviously, both CDs (left) and CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2 (right) were shown to blue fluorescence, suggesting that the CDs was the light source in CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2.

In addition, the fluorescence emission spectra of CDs and CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2 were obtained to further confirm the immobilization of CDs. As shown in Fig. 2F, when the excitation wavelength was 370 nm, the maximum emission peak of both the CDs and CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2 appeared at 448 nm, indicating that CDs were immobilized on Au-PEI@TiO2 successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL and EIS stepwise characterization of the biosensor assembly process
ce:section.ce:para: To characterize the stepwise preparation of the ECL biosensor, ECL measurements were conducted on MPI-A in 2 mL PBS containing 10 μM H2O2. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, the CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2 modified GCE produced high ECL intensity (a), indicating that the prepared CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2 had good ECL performance. After the incubation of H1, the ECL intensity was decreased (b) due to the fact that negatively charged DNA could hinder the electron transfer. Then, MCH was incubated to block the nonspecific binding sites, resulting in the decrease of ECL intensity (c) due to its hindrance to electron transfer. Afterward, when H2-DA and miRNA-21 were incubated on the electrode simultaneously, the ECL intensity was remarkably decreased (d). The reason was that abundant DA was introduced in electrode surface, which could quench the ECL intensity of CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2. All the results indicated that the proposed biosensor was successfully prepared.

To further confirm the successful fabrication of the biosensor, EIS is a useful way to characterize the fabrication process step by step. For EIS, the electron transfer resistance (Ret ), a key parameter for the electron transfer kinetics at the electrode interface, is approximate to semicircle diameter. The EIS profiles for the stepwise modified process of the electrode were recorded in [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− (5 mM) solution containing KCl (0.1 M). From Fig. 3B, the bare GCE showed a small Ret (a). After that, the Ret kept increasing when CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2 (b), H1 (c), MCH (d) and the mixture of miRNA-21 and H2-DA (e) were continuously modified onto the electrode. The gradual increasing of impedance values was due to the fact that the semiconductor TiO2, and negatively charged DNA and MCH could hinder the electron transfer from [Fe(CN)6]4−/3− to the electrode surface. These results demonstrated the successful construction of biosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ECL performance of the biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The detection performance of biosensor was analyzed by incubating a gradient concentration of miRNA-21 and H2-DA at the same time on the prepared sensor surface. In Fig.4 A, the ECL response was gradually decreased as the concentration of miRNA-21 increasing from 0.1 fM to 10 pM. The calibration curve showed a good linear relationship between the logarithm of the concentration (Fig. 4B). The linear equation was I = -18669.69–1717.90lg(c/M), the linear correlation coefficient was 0.9965, and the detection limit was 0.03 fM. The above results showed that the prepared sensor had better analytical performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the biosensor in cancer cells
ce:section.ce:para: In order to test the application value of the sensor, we completed these experiments in cancer cells. According the literatures [25,26], miRNA-21 was highly expressed in human breast cancer cells (MCF-7 cells). Keeping other conditions unchanged, the standard miRNA-21 samples were replaced with gradient cell number of MCF-7. The results were shown in Fig. 5 . It can be found that as the number of cells increased from 10 to 104, the corresponding ECL intensity decreased gradually. The above results showed that the sensor could be applied to the analysis of actual samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability and selectivity analysis of the biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: We also studied other important indicators for evaluating sensor performance, such as stability and selectivity. First, we performed an evaluation of the stability of the sensor by analyzing the changes in the three scans obtained by successive scans at different concentrations. As shown in Fig. 6 A, the ECL intensity of the three scan segments showed a slight decrease in the continuous scan, but the RSD values from the low to high concentrations were only 1.38%, 2.15%, 3.34%, 2.19% and 0.48%, respectively, indicating that the stability of the prepared sensor can meet the detection requirements.

Selectivity is another important indicator for evaluating sensor performance. Therefore, in this work, by replacing the target miRNA-21 with other miRNAs and keeping the other conditions unchanged, the ECL detection was performed, and the results of the selective evaluation were obtained. Other non-target microRNAs used in this work include the following: miRNA-155, miRNA-141, and miRNA-199A. As shown in Fig. 6B, when the concentration of non-target miRNAs is 100 pM, their ECL intensity values were also much higher than that of 1 pM miRNA-21, indicating that the sensor excellent selectivity.

In this work, a novel ECL biosensor based on self-catalytic CDs-Au-PEI@TiO2 nanoparticles and target-triggered CHA was constructed for sensitive detection of miRNA-21. Importantly, PEI could not only encapsulate TiO2 to act as reducing agent for in-situ reduction of HAuCl4 on its surface, but also significantly promote the ECL-generating properties. Noteworthy, Au-PEI@TiO2 was employed as carrier nanomaterials to achieve stable and efficient fixation of CDs with strong initial ECL signal, enhancing the detection sensitivity. Furthermore, with the efficient amplification of CHA, a large number of quenching groups were introduced into the sensing surface to obtain efficient quenching of the initial ECL signal, which further increasing the detection sensitivity of the sensor. Moreover, the constructed sensor can also show good results in actual sample application, and it shows great potential application value in future basic research work and early clinical diagnosis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation (NNSF) of China (21904108, 21775124, 21675129), the Natural Science Foundation Project of CQ CSTC (cstc2018jcyjAX0546), the Chongqing Research Program of Basic Research and Frontier Technology (cstc2017jcyjAX0230), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (XDJK2018C041, XDJK2019TJ002), China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.127490.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile microwave avenue to electrochemiluminescent two-color graphene quantum dots

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dot capped gold nanoflowers for electrochemiluminescent aptasensor of thrombin

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. You, W. Lin, H. Wu, Y.Q. Dong, Y.W. Chi, Carbon dot capped gold nanoflowers for electrochemiluminescent aptasensor of thrombin. Carbon, 127 (2018) 653-657.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots with red-shifted photoluminescence by fluorine doping for optical bio-imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.L. Gao, Y.M. Zhou, C.G. Xua, M.X. Guo, Z.C. Qia, X.J. Peng, B. Gao, Bright hydrophilic and organophilic ﬂuorescence carbon dots: One-pot fabricationandmulti-functional applications at visualized Au3+ detection in cell and white light-emitting devices. Sensor Actuat. B-Chem. 281 (2019) 905-911.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ferrocyanide-ferricyanide redox couple induced electrochemiluminescence amplification of carbon dots for ultrasensitive sensing of glutathione

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W.J. Niu, R.H. Zhu, S. Cosnier, X.J. Zhang, D. Shan, Ferrocyanide-Ferricyanide Redox Couple Induced Electrochemiluminescence Amplification of Carbon Dots for Ultrasensitive Sensing of Glutathione, Anal. Chem. 87 (2015) 11150-11156.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Paper-based electrochemiluminescence origami device for protein detection using assembled cascade DNA-carbon dots nanotags based on rolling circle amplification

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.D. Wu, C. Ma, X.X. Zheng, H.Y. Liu, J.H. Yu, Paper-based electrochemiluminescence origami device for protein detection using assembled cascade DNA-carbon dots nanotags based on rolling circle amplification, Biosens. Bioelectron. 68 (2015) 413-420.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Supersensitive detection of chlorinated phenols by multiple amplification electrochemiluminescence sensing based on carbon quantum Dots/Graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.L. Yang, J.S. Liang, S.L. Luo, C.B. Liu, Y.Y. Tang, Supersensitive Detection of Chlorinated Phenols by Multiple Amplification Electrochemiluminescence Sensing Based on Carbon Quantum Dots/Graphene, Anal. Chem. 85 (2013) 7720-7725.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemiluminescent graphene quantum dots as a sensing platform: a dual amplification for microRNA assay

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Zhang, Y. Zhuo, Y.Y. Chang, R. Yuan, Y.Q. Chai, Electrochemiluminescent graphene quantum dots as a sensing platform: a dual amplification for microRNA assay. Anal. Chem. 87 (2015) 10385-10391.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemiluminescence of nitrogen- and sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reduced carbon dots versus oxidized carbon dots: photo‐and electrochemiluminescence investigations for selected applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Xu, M. Wu, X.Z. Feng, X.B. Yin, X.W. He, Y.K. Zhang, Reduced Carbon Dots versus Oxidized Carbon Dots: Photo‐and Electrochemiluminescence Investigations for Selected Applications, Chem. Eur. J. 19 (2013) 6282-6288.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.T. Zheng, A. Ananthanarayanan, K.Q. Luo, P. Chen, Glowing Graphene Quantum Dots and Carbon Dots: Properties, Syntheses, and Biological Applications, Small 11 (2015) 1620-1636.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fabrication of carbon quantum dots and their application for efficient detecting Ru(bpy)3 2+ in the solution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z.H. Xu, J.G. Yu, G. Liu, Fabrication of carbon quantum dots and their application for efficient detecting Ru(bpy)32+ in the solution. Sensor Actuat. B-Chem. 181 (2013) 209-214.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ternary electrochemiluminescence system based on Rubrene Microrods as Luminophore and Pt nanomaterials as coreaction accelerator for ultrasensitive detection of MicroRNA from Cancer cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.L. Liu, Z.L. Tang, Y. Zhuo, Y.Q. Chai, R. Yuan, Ternary Electrochemiluminescence System Based on Rubrene Microrods as Luminophore and Pt Nanomaterials as Coreaction Accelerator for Ultrasensitive Detection of MicroRNA from Cancer Cells, Anal. Chem. 89 (2018) 9108-9115.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrasensitive immunoassay based on a pseudobienzyme amplifying system of choline oxidase and luminol-reduced Pt@Au hybrid nanoflowers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Zhou, Y. Zhuo, N. Liao, Y.Q. Chai, R. Yuan, Ultrasensitive immunoassay based on a pseudobienzyme amplifying system of choline oxidase and luminol-reduced Pt@Au hybrid nanoflowers. Chem. Comm. 50 (2014) 14627-14630.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An innovative ECL sensor based on AuNPs linker for Ru(bpy)3 2+ molecules doped onto an alkaline pretreated poly(4-aminodiphenylamine) film

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.M. Al-Hinaai, H.H. Kyaw, S.H. Al-Harthi, E.A. Khudaish, An innovative ECL sensor based on AuNPs linker for Ru(bpy)32+ molecules doped onto an alkaline pretreated poly(4-aminodiphenylamine) film. Sensor Actuat. B-Chem. 257 (2018) 460-468.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel aptasensor for the detection of adenosine in cancer cells by electrochemiluminescence of nitrogen doped TiO2 nanotubes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.Y. Tian, J.J. Xu, H.Y. Chen, A novel aptasensor for the detection of adenosine in cancer cells by electrochemiluminescence of nitrogen doped TiO2 nanotubes, Chem. Commun. 48 (2012) 8234-8236.

Lin, Fluoro-coumarin silicon phthalocyanine sensitized integrated electrochemiluminescence bioprobe constructed on TiO2 MOFs for the sensing of deoxynivalenol, Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem. 269 (2018) 27-35.
H.H. Duan, D.S. Wang, Y.D. Li, Green chemistry for nanoparticle synthesis, Chem. Soc. Rev. 44 (2015) 5778-5792.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hybrid films of polyetherimide containing in situ grown Ag, Pd, and AgPd alloy nanoparticles: synthesis route, morphology, and gas transport properties

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Simon, P. Alcouffe, E. Espuche, Hybrid films of polyetherimide containing in situ grown Ag, Pd, and AgPd alloy nanoparticles: Synthesis route, morphology, and gas transport properties, J Polym. Sci. 52 (2014) 1211-1220.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Evaporation-induced surface coating of poly (p-phenylene benzobisoxazole) fibers with polyetherimide encapsulated nano-TiO2

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Zhu, B. Liu, L.M. Bao, Evaporation-induced surface coating of poly (p-phenylene benzobisoxazole) fibers with polyetherimide encapsulated nano-TiO2, Prog. Org. Coat. 116 (2018) 43-50.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical sensing based MWCNT-Cobalt tetra substituted sorbaamide phthalocyanine onto the glassy carbon electrode towards the determination of 2-Amino phenol: A voltammetric study

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.S. Jilani, C.D. Mruthyunjayachari, P. Malathesh, T.M. Sharankumar, K.V. Reddy, Electrochemical sensing based MWCNT-Cobalt tetra substituted sorbaamide phthalocyanine onto the glassy carbon electrode towards the determination of 2-Amino phenol: A voltammetric study. Sensor. Actuat. B-Chem. 301 (2019) 127078.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid and sensitive detection of microRNA via the capture of fluorescent dyes-loaded albumin nanoparticles around functionalized magnetic beads

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.X. Wei, D. Du, Z.Y. Wang, W.W. Zhang, Y.H. Lin, Z.H. Dai, Rapid and sensitive detection of microRNA via the capture of fluorescent dyes-loaded albumin nanoparticles around functionalized magnetic beads, Biosens. Bioelectron. 94 (2017) 56-62.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Catalytic molecular imaging of microRNA in living cells by DNA-programmed nanoparticle disassembly

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. He, T. Zeng, Z. Li, G. Wang, N. Ma, Catalytic molecular imaging of microRNA in living cells by DNA-programmed nanoparticle disassembly, Angew. Chem. Int. Edit. 55 (2016) 3073-3076.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xia Zhong is senior experimentalist in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Southwest University, China. She received her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from this university. Her main research interest is electrochemiluminescent and electrochemical biosensors.
Xue Li is MS candidate in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Southwest University, China. Her main research interest is electrochemiluminescent biosensors.

Ying Zhuo is a professor in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Southwest University, China. She received her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from this university. Her main research interests are the electrochemiluminescent and electrochemical biosensors.

Yaqin Chai is a professor in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Southwest University, China. She received her Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from this university. Her main research interests are the electrochemiluminescent and electrochemical biosensors.

Ruo Yuan is a professor in the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Southwest University, China. He received his Ph.D. degree in analytical chemistry from Hunan University. His main research interests are the electrochemiluminescent and electrochemical biosensors.
